inline_review:
commit 9274f456bc50e961be34dec22bc669ec97dc9733
Author: Donet Tom <donettom@linux.ibm.com>
sched/numa, mm: Skip page promotion if cpu pid is valid

This patch attempts to prevent unnecessary page promotion when memory tiering
is enabled at runtime by skipping promotion if the folio's last_cpupid
contains a valid CPU and PID instead of an access timestamp.

> diff --git a/kernel/sched/fair.c b/kernel/sched/fair.c
> index 226509231e673..91e6dc6010af9 100644
> --- a/kernel/sched/fair.c
> +++ b/kernel/sched/fair.c
[ ... ]
> @@ -2071,6 +2071,13 @@ bool should_numa_migrate_memory(struct task_struct *p, struct folio *folio,
>  		unsigned int latency, th, def_th;
>  		long nr = folio_nr_pages(folio);
>  
> +		/* When tiering is enabled at runtime, last_cpupid may
> +		 * hold a valid cpupid instead of an access timestamp.
> +		 * If so, skip page promotion.
> +		 */
> +		if (cpupid_valid(folio_last_cpupid(folio)))
> +			return false;

Could this check unintentionally match valid access timestamps, permanently
disabling promotion for some folios?

When memory tiering is enabled, the folio's _last_cpupid field is repurposed
to store an access timestamp derived from jiffies_to_msecs(jiffies). The
cpupid_valid() function checks if the extracted CPU bits are less than
nr_cpu_ids.

Since the timestamp is continuously increasing based on jiffies, won't its
bits frequently alias to a value that cpupid_valid() considers a valid
CPU ID? On systems where nr_cpu_ids equals the maximum value allowed by the
CPU mask, it seems this check would always evaluate to true.

Furthermore, by returning false early, the code bypasses the call to
numa_hint_fault_latency() which is responsible for updating the timestamp.
Does this leave the aliasing timestamp stuck in the folio without being
updated, permanently preventing its promotion?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"The patch conflates access timestamps with CPUPIDs, breaking memory tiering page promotion.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. When memory tiering is enabled (`folio_use_access_time(folio)` is true), the `folio->_last_cpupid` field is repurposed to store an access timestamp derived from `jiffies_to_msecs(jiffies)`.
2. The patch introduces `cpupid_valid(folio_last_cpupid(folio))` to skip promotion if a valid CPU/PID pair from before tiering was enabled is detected. However, access timestamps and CPUPIDs share the exact same bit space without any discriminating tag.
3. `cpupid_valid()` extracts the 'CPU ID' bits from the integer and checks if the value is `< nr_cpu_ids`. Because the timestamp is derived from continuously increasing jiffies, its bits will frequently alias to a valid CPU ID. 
4. If `nr_cpu_ids` equals the maximum value allowed by the CPU mask (e.g., 256 CPUs on an 8-bit mask), `cpupid_valid()` will always evaluate to true for any timestamp, completely and permanently disabling NUMA promotion for all slow-node memory system-wide.
5. On systems with fewer CPUs, it will still evaluate to true for large, periodic windows. Furthermore, by returning `false` early, the function bypasses `numa_hint_fault_latency()`, which is responsible for updating the timestamp. This leaves the aliasing timestamp stuck in the folio, permanently preventing its promotion."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 9274f456bc50e961be34dec22bc669ec97dc9733

> +		 * hold a valid cpupid instead of an access timestamp.
